customer_goal: Make rich-output Gaussian rendering faster for independently moving robotics cameras without weakening fidelity, bounded-memory behavior, or CUDA safety.

verdict.summary: Reusing first-pass projection candidates removes the second full camera/Gaussian projection. On the full 21.5M-Gaussian Home scene at logical B1024/P8 it improves 128x128 throughput by 40.41% and 256x256 throughput by 10.35%. On the separate 13.9M-Gaussian Garage 72-view B8/256x256 ABBA control it improves 4.58%. The benefit is therefore real but workload-dependent.
verdict.batch_scaling: The full Home 128x128 candidate remains flat from physical P1 through P32: 198.30 images/s at P1 and 193.83 images/s at P32. This optimization reduces repeated work; it does not produce proportional vectorization scaling.
verdict.memory: The project-once path stores every first-pass pixel-contributing candidate rather than only depth-retained records. Workspace rises by 55.01% on Home 128x128 P8, 40.64% on Home 256x256 P8, and 5.36% on Garage 256x256 B8. A tested memory-ceiling fallback preserves the direct-reprojection path.
verdict.claim_boundary: These are direct standalone L4 results for RGB, expected depth, alpha, and semantic ID. They are not end-to-end Isaac Sim, Kit, Fabric, OVRTX, RTX, L40, RGB-only, or universal Gaussian-renderer results.

source.baseline: The frozen 51bc5a1 retained-record implementation projects every camera/Gaussian pair in both compact passes and stores 32-byte records only for candidates surviving at least one per-pixel depth cutoff.
source.candidate: Pass 1 projects once, accumulates per-pixel depth-bucket optical thickness, and stores a 44-byte record for every projection that contributes alpha to at least one pixel. A separate kernel emits cutoff-retained intersections from those records, and compositing consumes the same records.

pipeline: Project each active camera/Gaussian pair once, accumulate conservative optical thickness, and append a bounded projected candidate if it contributes to any pixel.
Derive each pixel's conservative retained-depth cutoff.
Visit stored projected candidates and emit only cutoff-retained pixel intersections.
Globally radix-sort the emitted prefix by pixel and depth.
Composite RGB, expected depth, alpha, and semantic ID from the stored projection records.
implementation.bounded_fallback: If the complete 44-byte candidate workspace exceeds max_workspace_bytes, telemetry reports workspace-memory-ceiling and the renderer runs the prior direct-reprojection path.

protocol.logical_home: Every observation covers 1,024 distinct cameras exactly once through bounded physical microbatches; all 1,024 cameras change between the two measured steps.
protocol.home_scene: Full 21,497,908-Gaussian Home Scan, independently moving cameras, spatially coherent semantic classes, RGB+depth+alpha+semantic output.
protocol.home_timing: CUDA events and synchronized wall time around a complete logical B1024 observation. Scene load, JIT, warmup, validation, output encoding, and capacity retries are outside measured ranges.
protocol.home_p8_samples: One frozen baseline process and one candidate process, six measured logical-batch samples per process after warmup and full-output validation.
protocol.garage_scene: Full 13,943,336-Gaussian Garage High Detail scene, immutable 72-view B8/256x256 contract, spatially coherent semantic classes.
protocol.garage_timing: Each process warms five batches, runs complete trajectory repetitions for at least three measured seconds, and covers all 72 source views per repetition.
protocol.gsplat_control: A same-node pinned-gsplat control was attempted from a clean extension cache under a hard 30-minute timeout. CUDA extension compilation consumed the window and exited 124 before rendering, so no gsplat throughput or comparison is reported from this run.

home_full_scene.scaling_interpretation: No proportional physical-batch scaling was gained. P1 is the fastest tested point; larger microbatches primarily increase workspace.

profile.case: Full Home Scan, logical B1024, physical P8, 128x128, two measured steps and 256 native submissions.
profile.interpretation: The expensive projection kernel is called once rather than twice per native submission. It is replaced by one cheaper cached-emission launch, so total launch count does not fall. Combined projection plus cached emission takes 60.00% of the old two-pass projection time; total kernel time falls 30.35%. Sorting time is effectively unchanged and becomes the next largest stage.
profile.ncu_limitation: Hardware counters were not claimed; the previous node preflight reported RmProfilingAdminOnly=1.

safety_and_contracts.unit_tests: 172 passed, 1 skipped after the profiler-classification regression test was added.

isaac_kit_fabric.reason: The bounded GCP standalone L4 image does not contain Isaac Sim. Standalone CUDA evidence is not relabeled as integration evidence.

upstream_research.conclusion: No exact drop-in kernel was found for project-once candidate compaction plus project-specific per-pixel optical-thickness cutoff, cutoff-filtered pixel intersection emission, global depth ordering, and fused RGB/depth/alpha/semantic output. This is a bounded search result, not proof that no implementation exists.
upstream_research.reused_architecture: Project once and consume stored screen-space records downstream.

nearby_implementations.finding: Provides generic scan, selection, run-length, and radix-sort primitives, not the renderer-specific projection/cutoff/emission operation.

Uses count and write passes and recomputes projection; its downstream tile intersection consumes stored screen-space records but does not implement this renderer's per-pixel optical-thickness cutoff contract.

Projects once into dense records and duplicates tile instances after a scan. That architecture is relevant, but its dense storage and tile-level contract are not a drop-in replacement for this pixel-cutoff rich-output path.

Fuses projection with precise tile-intersection generation using a different tile-mask pipeline. Its published speedups are not transferable to this renderer without changing and revalidating the algorithmic contract.

limitations: This is L4 evidence, not the requested final L40 hardware gate.
The benchmark bypasses Isaac Sim, Kit, Fabric, OVRTX, and RTX.
The strongest gain is the low-resolution projection-heavy Home case; separate 256x256 gains are 4.58% to 10.35%.
The candidate increases workspace because first-pass contributors can substantially outnumber depth-retained records.
The physical-batch sweep does not demonstrate vectorization scaling.
No new RGB-only comparison is included; all headline renderer timings require RGB, expected depth, alpha, and semantic ID.
The optional same-node pinned-gsplat control timed out during its clean CUDA extension build and produced no renderer measurement.
Nsight Compute occupancy, divergence, and memory-throughput counters were unavailable.
next_work: Run the finite Isaac headless, Kit extension, and Fabric camera ladder on an Isaac-capable node.
Repeat the qualified cases on one L40 for the requested hardware gate.
Add a measured selection policy so project-once reuse is enabled only when its speed/memory tradeoff is favorable and the complete workspace fits.
Reduce candidate-record or overlaunch overhead without weakening exact cutoff and output semantics.
Profile sorting and compositing next; do not describe either as the sole batching bottleneck without a new A/B.
